After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misao Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misao Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.

'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.

Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misao Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.

The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.

It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misao Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misao Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|ja|カナメモチ}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.

'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.

In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.

revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|ja|カナメモチ}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.

revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|ja|カナメモチ}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.

revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|ja|カナメモチ}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office== See Also == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|ja|大西祝}} - A philosopher from Okayama

revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|ja|カナメモチ}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office == See Also! == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|ja|大西祝}} - A philosopher from Okayama
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Mount Misao}} * [http://www.okayama-park.or.jp/ Okayama City Parks Association]

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|ja|カナメモチ}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>

revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|ja|カナメモチ}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office == See Also == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|ja|大西祝}} - A philosopher from Okayama
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Mount Misao}} * [http://www.okayama-park.or.jp/ Okayama City Parks Association]

revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市|12=draft}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)|12=draft}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)|12=draft}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園|12=draft}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|12=draft}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|12=draft}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|12=draft}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|12=draft}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|12=draft}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|12=draft}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|12=draft}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|12=draft}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林|12=draft}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|12=draft}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|12=draft}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|ja|カナメモチ|12=draft}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>

The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|12=draft}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|12=draft}}.

revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市|12=draft}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)|12=draft}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)|12=draft}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園|12=draft}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|12=draft}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|12=draft}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|12=draft}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|12=draft}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|12=draft}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|12=draft}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|12=draft}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|12=draft}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林|12=draft}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|12=draft}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|12=draft}}.

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)|12=draft|12=draft}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)|12=draft|12=draft}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園|12=draft|12=draft}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|12=draft|12=draft}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|12=draft|12=draft}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft|12=draft}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|12=draft|12=draft}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|12=draft|12=draft}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|12=draft|12=draft}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林|12=draft|12=draft}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|12=draft|12=draft}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft|12=draft}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|12=draft|12=draft}}.

revision.comment: 18 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:操山 up to rev 92562591
revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |画像 = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |画像キャプション = <small>岡山平野と操山</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[岡山県]][[岡山市]][[中区 (岡山市)|中区]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>操山の位置</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''操山'''（みさおやま）は[[岡山県]][[岡山市]][[中区 (岡山市)|中区]]にある[[山|低山]]。標高は169mである。
なお「そうざん」と訓じた場合、操山西北一帯の地名を指す。
かつては、'''三棹山'''や'''三櫂山'''とも表記されていた。 ==概要== 岡山市街地の東部にある低山で、[[後楽園]]の借景となっている。操山の169[[メートル|m]]を最高地点に東に向かって円山（138m）、笠井山（134m）が連なる。
操山は古来、瓶井山（みかいやま）と呼ばれていた。[[江戸時代]]になり三櫂山もしくは三棹山と呼ばれるようになった。のち、操山と表記されるようになった。古代祭祀が行われた場所であり、山上には[[磐座]]があり、[[古墳]]が多数点在し[[操山古墳群]]を形成している。
山麓には[[曹源寺 (岡山市)|曹源寺]]、仏心寺、[[岡山県護国神社]]、[[少林寺 (岡山市)|少林寺]]、[[安住院]]、[[恩徳寺 (岡山市)|恩徳寺]]などの神社仏閣が点在する。また、丘陵上には[[吉備津岡辛木神社]]、円山不動明王、三勲神社跡、[[明善寺合戦|明禅寺城跡]]など神社・遺跡がある。
江戸時代には[[岡山藩]]の藩有林となっていた。[[明治]]以後は[[国有林]]となり現在に至っている。 丘陵には遊歩道が整備され、操山公園となっている。
== 操山公園里山センター == [[平成]]11年（[[1999年]]）に建造された里山を学ぶ施設である。操山に関する情報・書物・展示物などを備えており、ふれあいスペース・会議室・多目的ホールが設けられている。 また、周囲には、ふれあい広場、炭焼小屋、農園、果樹園が設置されている。 自然観察会、自然・歴史講座などのイベントも開催される。
==三勲神社跡地== '''三勲神社'''（さんくんじんじゃ）は、操山の西に延びる稜線の端にあった神社である。明治8年（[[1875年]]）、国の施策のもと地元有志の手により、岡山市街が眺望出来る場所に創建された。祭神は[[和気清麻呂]]、[[楠木正行]]、[[児島高徳]]の三勲。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は県社。有志による創建であったため氏子が存在しなかった。このため[[太平洋戦争]]後、[[近代社格制度]]の廃止により神社の運営が不可能となり、[[昭和]]22年（[[1947年]]）[[玉井宮東照宮]]の境内に遷座した。

revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |画像 = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |画像キャプション = <small>岡山平野と操山</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[岡山県]][[岡山市]][[中区 (岡山市)|中区]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>操山の位置</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''操山'''（みさおやま）は[[岡山県]][[岡山市]][[中区 (岡山市)|中区]]にある[[山|低山]]。標高は169mである。
なお「そうざん」と訓じた場合、操山西北一帯の地名を指す。
かつては、'''三棹山'''や'''三櫂山'''とも表記されていた。 ==概要== 岡山市街地の東部にある低山で、[[後楽園]]の借景となっている。操山の169[[メートル|m]]を最高地点に東に向かって円山（138m）、笠井山（134m）が連なる。
操山は古来、瓶井山（みかいやま）と呼ばれていた。[[江戸時代]]になり三櫂山もしくは三棹山と呼ばれるようになった。のち、操山と表記されるようになった。古代祭祀が行われた場所であり、山上には[[磐座]]があり、[[古墳]]が多数点在し[[操山古墳群]]を形成している。
山麓には[[曹源寺 (岡山市)|曹源寺]]、仏心寺、[[岡山県護国神社]]、[[少林寺 (岡山市)|少林寺]]、[[安住院]]、[[恩徳寺 (岡山市)|恩徳寺]]などの神社仏閣が点在する。また、丘陵上には[[吉備津岡辛木神社]]、円山不動明王、三勲神社跡、[[明善寺合戦|明禅寺城跡]]など神社・遺跡がある。
江戸時代には[[岡山藩]]の藩有林となっていた。[[明治]]以後は[[国有林]]となり現在に至っている。 丘陵には遊歩道が整備され、操山公園となっている。
== 操山公園里山センター == [[平成]]11年（[[1999年]]）に建造された里山を学ぶ施設である。操山に関する情報・書物・展示物などを備えており、ふれあいスペース・会議室・多目的ホールが設けられている。 また、周囲には、ふれあい広場、炭焼小屋、農園、果樹園が設置されている。 自然観察会、自然・歴史講座などのイベントも開催される。
==三勲神社跡地== '''三勲神社'''（さんくんじんじゃ）は、操山の西に延びる稜線の端にあった神社である。明治8年（[[1875年]]）、国の施策のもと地元有志の手により、岡山市街が眺望出来る場所に創建された。祭神は[[和気清麻呂]]、[[楠木正行]]、[[児島高徳]]の三勲。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は県社。有志による創建であったため氏子が存在しなかった。このため[[太平洋戦争]]後、[[近代社格制度]]の廃止により神社の運営が不可能となり、[[昭和]]22年（[[1947年]]）[[玉井宮東照宮]]の境内に遷座した。
== ギャラリー == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|操山丘陵全景 File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|操山山頂 File:Maruyama Top.JPG|円山山頂 File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|笠井山山頂 File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|操山公園里山センター File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|遊歩道 File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|[[カナメモチ]]のトンネル道 File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|竹林の遊歩道 File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|三勲神社跡地 File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|円山不動明王 File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|吉備津岡辛木神社 File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|明禅寺城址 File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|三勲神社跡地から望む岡山市街 File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|旗振台展望所から望む岡山平野 </gallery>

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.

== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office

== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園|qq=draft}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|qq=draft}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|qq=draft}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|qq=draft}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林|qq=draft}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft}}.

== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
