== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

[[lld:Mont Misao]] [[ja:操山]] [[sn:Mount Misao]] [[en:Mount Misao]]
<!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao-->

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama City|qq=}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka Ward (Okayama City)|qq=}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama City|qq=}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka Ward (Okayama City)|qq=}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korakuen|qq=}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|qq=}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|qq=}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura (Shinto)|qq=}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|qq=}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|qq=}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|qq=}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|qq=}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|qq=}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|qq=}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|qq=}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|qq=}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:岡山市中区の地理]] into [[Category:Geography of Naka Ward, Okayama City]] via bot
revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|qq=}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

<!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--> [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] [[Category:Geography of Naka Ward, Okayama City]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Mount Misao}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11498770]] Wikidata name: Mount Misao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|qq=|1=Maruyama (Okayama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394758}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|qq=|1=Sōgen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515227}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=|1=Ontokuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492251}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=|1=Kibitsuokakaraki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412651}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=|1=Battle of Myōzenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512040}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.

<!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--><!--Mount Misao--> [[Category:Mountain Gazetteers]] [[Category:Geography of Naka Ward, Okayama City]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Mount Misao]][[Category:qq]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Mount Misao}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11498770]] Wikidata name: Mount Misao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|qq=|1=Maruyama (Okayama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394758}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|qq=|1=Sōgen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515227}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=|1=Ontoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492251}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=|1=Kibitsuokakaraki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412651}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=|1=Battle of Myōzenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512040}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.

It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11498770]] Wikidata name: Mount Misao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|qq=|1=Maruyama (Okayama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394758}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|qq=|1=Sōgen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515227}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=|1=Ontoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492251}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=|1=Kibitsuokakaraki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412651}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=|1=Battle of Myōzenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512040}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.

At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|qq=|1=Sōgen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515227}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=|1=Ontoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492251}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=|1=Kibitsuokakaraki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412651}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=|1=Battle of Myōzenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512040}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.

'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.

Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.

'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|1=Maruyama (Okayama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394758}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|1=Sōgen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515227}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|1=Ontoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492251}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|1=Kibitsuokakaraki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412651}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|1=Battle of Myōzenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512040}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Misao]][[ja:操山]][[lld:Mont Misao]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11498770]] Wikidata name: Mount Misao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|1=Maruyama (Okayama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394758}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|1=Sōgen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515227}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|1=Ontoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492251}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|1=Kibitsuokakaraki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412651}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|1=Battle of Myōzenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512040}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Misao]][[ja:操山]][[lld:Mont Misao]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11498770]] Wikidata name: Mount Misao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|1=Maruyama (Okayama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394758}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|1=Sōgen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515227}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|1=Ontoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492251}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|1=Kibitsuokakaraki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412651}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|1=Battle of Myōzenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512040}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of [[:en:Photinia glabra|Photinia]] File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>

It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|1=Sōgen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515227}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|1=Ontoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492251}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|1=Kibitsuokakaraki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412651}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|1=Battle of Myōzenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512040}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Misao]][[ja:操山]][[lld:Mont Misao]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11498770]] Wikidata name: Mount Misao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|1=Maruyama (Okayama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394758}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|1=Sōgen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515227}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|1=Ontoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492251}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|1=Kibitsuokakaraki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412651}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|1=Battle of Myōzenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512040}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mount Misao]][[ja:操山]][[lld:Mont Misao]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11498770]] Wikidata name: Mount Misao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |image = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |imageキャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]][[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]][[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]] |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama City]], [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka Ward (Okayama City)]]. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]]. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama (Okayama)|1=Maruyama (Okayama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394758}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaiyama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rocks) and numerous [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misaoyama Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaoyama Kofun Group|qq=}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|1=Sōgen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11515227}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|1=Ontoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11492251}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|1=Kibitsuokakaraki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412651}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|1=Battle of Myōzenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11512040}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], later becoming a [[:en:National Forest|State forest]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were [[:en:Wake no Kiyomaro|Wake no Kiyomaro]], [[:en:Kusunoki Masatsura|Kusunoki Masatsura]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|1=Kojima Takanori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10855355}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|1=Tamaigu Tōshō-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572375}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of [[:en:Photinia glabra|Photinia]] File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office
